3A_Bibliographic.citation: Zink, J., O'Connor, S. G., Blachman-Demner, D. R., Wolff-Hughes, D. L., & Berrigan, D. (2024). Examining the bidirectional associations between sleep duration, screen time, and internalizing symptoms in the ABCD Study. Journal of Adolescent Health, 74, 496-503.

3B_Sample.timepoints_used: Baseline (ages ~9-10) and 1-year follow-up (ages ~10-11), pre-COVID data only

3C_Exposure.exposure_measure: Youth Screen Time Survey (weekday and weekend separately) and caregiver-reported sleep duration
3C_Exposure.how_operationalized: Screen time: sum of hours across 6 types (viewing/streaming TV/movies, YouTube, video games, texting, video chat, social networking) for weekday and weekend separately; dichotomized as >2 hrs/day vs <=2 hrs/day. Sleep duration: caregiver-reported, dichotomized as <9 hrs vs 9-11 hrs/night.

3D_Outcome.measure_used: Child Behavior Checklist (CBCL/4-18) subscales: withdrawn/depressed, anxious/depressed, somatic complaints; T-scores dichotomized at >=65 (borderline clinical) vs <65
3D_Outcome.base_rate_if_binary: Females at baseline: withdrawn/depressed 4.6%, anxious/depressed 7.5%, somatic complaints 8.7%; Males: 9.4%, 7.3%, 7.4% respectively

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: SAS GLIMMIX with study site as random effect. Forward models: baseline sleep duration + screen time (weekday and weekend) as simultaneous predictors of each internalizing subscale at follow-up. Reverse models: baseline internalizing symptoms predicting follow-up sleep/screen time. Interaction terms tested but not significant. Gender-stratified a priori. Adjusted for covariates and baseline outcome variable.
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: Age, race/ethnicity, income-to-needs ratio (SES), BMI percentile, physical activity, baseline outcome
3E_Design_Analysis.SES_in_model: yes - income-to-needs ratio included as covariate, categorized as poverty (<=0.99), low income (1.00-1.99), intermediate (2.00-3.99), high (>=4.00)
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: To adjust for the clustering of observations within study sites, multilevel logistic regressions with the study site specified as a random effect were used to test the associations of interest using the SAS GLIMMIX procedure.

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Females: sleep 9-11 hrs (vs <9 hrs) -> withdrawn/depressed at follow-up (adjusted)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: In females, 9-11 hours/night of sleep at baseline was protective against withdrawn/depressed symptoms (odds ratio [OR] 0.6, 95% confidence interval [CI] 0.4-0.8)

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Females: sleep 9-11 hrs (vs <9 hrs) -> somatic complaints at follow-up (adjusted)
Females: weekend screen time >2 hrs (vs <=2 hrs) -> withdrawn/depressed at follow-up (adjusted)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: In females, greater baseline weekend screen time was associated with increased risk of withdrawn/depressed symptoms (OR 1.6, 95% CI 1.1-2.2, p = .0129)

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Males: no significant associations for screen time or sleep predicting internalizing symptoms
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: No other significant associations were observed [in males].

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Baseline internalizing symptoms did not predict screen time or sleep at follow-up in either gender
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Lastly, being at or above borderline clinical levels of withdrawn/depressed symptoms (vs. not) at baseline was unrelated to subsequent sleep duration and screen time (on weekdays and weekend days) in females and males.

3H_Rhetorical.pattern_detail: The paper presents a balanced interpretation. The significant OR of 1.6 for weekend screen time predicting withdrawn/depressed symptoms in females is a moderate effect. The paper correctly notes that most associations were null (no male effects, no reverse causation, no weekday screen time effects, no interaction effects). No inflated clinical recommendations are made.

3I_Flags.ambiguity_notes: The key finding is narrow: only weekend screen time (not weekday) predicted only withdrawn/depressed symptoms (not anxiety or somatic) only in females (not males), and only unidirectionally. The study properly frames these as limited findings requiring further investigation. Sleep is the co-exposure, so this paper examines both sleep and screen time simultaneously. Baseline and follow-up are only 1 year apart at ages 9-11 (pre-COVID).
